company_profile.name: Arhaus, Inc.
company_profile.type_rationale: Standard company — balanced weighting across all methods
company_profile.description: Arhaus, Inc. operates as a premium retailer in the home furnishings market in the United States. The company offers bedroom, dining room, and living room, as well as home office furnishings, including sofas, dining tables and chairs, accent chairs, console and coffee tables, beds, headboards, dressers, desks, bookcases, modular storage, and other items; and outdoor products, such as outdoor dining tables, chairs, chaises and other furniture, lighting, textiles, décor, umbrellas, and fire pits. It also provides bath product assortment comprising vanities and storage pieces, faucets and hardware, and Turkish bath towels; lighting fixtures, including chandeliers, pendants, table and floor lamps, and sconces; indoor and outdoor rugs, bed linens, and pillows and throws; and décor products, such as wall art, mirrors, vases, candles, and other decorative accessories. The company distributes its products through an omni-channel model comprising showrooms, ecommerce, catalogs and digital media, interior designer services, trade services, and client engagement. Arhaus, Inc. was founded in 1986 and is headquartered in Boston Heights, Ohio.
company_profile.description_summary:
Arhaus operates as a premium home furnishings retailer offering bedroom, dining, living room, and home office furniture alongside outdoor products, lighting, rugs, bath accessories, and decorative items. The company generates revenue through an omni-channel distribution model encompassing physical showrooms, ecommerce, catalogs, and designer services.

The company differentiates itself through curated, higher-end product assortments and personalized interior design services that complement its retail channels.

company_profile.name: Arm Holdings plc
company_profile.type_rationale: Cyclical business (technology) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
company_profile.description: Arm Holdings plc researches, develops, licenses, and markets central processing unit (CPU) intellectual property (IP), graphics processing unit IP, systems IP, compute subsystems (CSS), and associated software, tools and related services. The company provides a product portfolio, including CPU IP, GPU and neural processing unit (NPU) accelerators, system IP such as interconnects, compute platform products including pre-integrated CSSs, and development tools and software. The company serves semiconductor companies, original equipment manufacturers (OEMs), cloud service providers (CSPs), and organizations developing chips for end markets such as smartphones, consumer electronics, industrial IoT, embedded systems, cloud data centers, networking, automotive, and robotics. It provides its products and services in the United States, China, Japan, Taiwan, Korea, and internationally. The company was founded in 1990 and is based in Cambridge, United Kingdom. Arm Holdings plc operates as a subsidiary of SoftBank Group Corp.
company_profile.description_summary:
This company researches, develops, and licenses processor intellectual property, including CPU, GPU, and neural processing unit designs, alongside associated software tools and services. It generates revenue by licensing these technologies to semiconductor companies, manufacturers, and cloud providers building chips for smartphones, data centers, automotive systems, and industrial applications across global markets.

The company's distinctive position stems from its foundational role in processor architecture design, enabling diverse industries to develop customized chips rather than building from scratch. Operating as a subsidiary of SoftBank Group, it maintains a geographically distributed customer base spanning major technology hubs worldwide.

_meta.type_rationale: Cyclical business (technology) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
_meta.method_explanation: Cyclical companies — such as semiconductors, mining, or manufacturing — have profits that rise sharply in good times and fall sharply in downturns. This makes earnings-based methods like P/E unreliable, because the earnings figure at any single point in time can be unusually high or low. EV/EBITDA smooths out some of this distortion by looking at earnings before interest and tax, making it a fairer comparison across the economic cycle. Price-to-Sales is also useful as revenue tends to be more stable than profits for cyclical businesses.

company_profile.name: Arvinas, Inc.
company_profile.type_rationale: Standard company — balanced weighting across all methods
company_profile.description: Arvinas, Inc., a clinical-stage biotechnology company, engages in the discovery, development, and commercialization of therapies to degrade disease-causing proteins. The company develops proteolysis targeting chimeras (PROTAC) targeted protein degraders that are designed to harness the body's own natural protein disposal system to degrade and remove disease-causing proteins. It is also developing clinical development programs , including ARV-102, a leucine-rich repeat kinase 2 (LRRK2) protein for the treatment of neurodegenerative diseases, such as Parkinson's disease and progressive supranuclear palsy; ARV-806, a Kirsten rat sarcoma, G12D protein for the treatment of cancers with the G12D mutation, comprising pancreatic, colorectal, and non-small cell lung cancer; ARV-393, a B-cell lymphoma 6 protein for the treatment of relapsed/refractory non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL); ARV-027, a polyglutamine-expanded androgen receptor in skeletal muscle; and vepdegestrant, an estrogen receptor for the treatment of locally advanced or metastatic ER+/HER2- breast cancer. In addition, the company develops Bavdegalutamide (ARV-110) and Luxdegalutamide (ARV-766), investigational orally bioavailable PROTAC protein degraders for the treatment of men with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer. It has collaborations with Pfizer Inc., Genentech, Inc., F. Hoffman-La Roche Ltd., Carrick Therapeutics Limited, and Bayer AG. The company was founded in 2013 and is based in New Haven, Connecticut.
company_profile.description_summary:
Arvinas is a clinical-stage biotechnology company developing proteolysis targeting chimeras (PROTACs) designed to degrade disease-causing proteins using the body's natural protein disposal system. The company has multiple clinical programs targeting neurodegenerative diseases, various cancers, and prostate cancer.

The company's distinctive approach leverages protein degradation technology rather than traditional protein inhibition, partnering with major pharmaceutical firms including Pfizer, Genentech, Roche, and Bayer to advance its pipeline.

company_profile.name: ASP Isotopes Inc.
company_profile.type_rationale: Cyclical business (basic materials) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
company_profile.description: ASP Isotopes Inc., a development stage advanced materials company, engages in the production, distribution, marketing, and sale of isotopes in South Africa,Hong Kong, and United States. It operates in two segments, Nuclear Fuels, and Specialist Isotopes and Related Services. The Nuclear Fuels segment focuses on the research and development of technologies and methods used to produce high-assay low-enriched uranium (HALEU) and Lithium-6 for the advanced nuclear fuels target end market. Its Specialist Isotopes and Related Services segment engages in the research and development of technologies and methods used to separate high-value, low-volume isotopes, such as C-14, Mo-100 and Si-28, for target end markets, including pharmaceuticals and agrochemicals, nuclear medical imaging, and semiconductors; provision of related services; and operation of PET labs. The company offers the Aerodynamic Separation Process (ASP) technology, which separates gas species and isotopes in a volatile state; and the Quantum Enrichment (QE) technology, which separates isotopes. It also produces and commercializes Molybdenum-100, Molybdenum-98, and Ytterbium-176 for the nuclear medical industry. In addition, the company develops Zinc-68, Nickel-64, and Xenon-129/136 for the nuclear medical industry; Lithium-7, Uranium-235, and Chlorine-37 for the energy industry; and Germanium-70/72/74 for semiconductor markets. The company was incorporated in 2021 and is based in Dallas, Texas.
company_profile.description_summary:
ASP Isotopes develops and produces specialized isotopes for nuclear, medical, and semiconductor applications across South Africa, Hong Kong, and the United States. Operating in two segments, it focuses on advanced nuclear fuels like HALEU and Lithium-6, plus high-value isotopes for pharmaceuticals, medical imaging, and semiconductors. The company commercializes proprietary separation technologies including its Aerodynamic Separation Process and Quantum Enrichment technology.

The company distinguishes itself through its diverse isotope portfolio and dual-technology platform, serving niche, high-value markets from nuclear medicine to semiconductor manufacturing, while operating PET imaging facilities alongside its production and research operations.

_meta.type_rationale: Cyclical business (basic materials) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
_meta.method_explanation: Cyclical companies — such as semiconductors, mining, or manufacturing — have profits that rise sharply in good times and fall sharply in downturns. This makes earnings-based methods like P/E unreliable, because the earnings figure at any single point in time can be unusually high or low. EV/EBITDA smooths out some of this distortion by looking at earnings before interest and tax, making it a fairer comparison across the economic cycle. Price-to-Sales is also useful as revenue tends to be more stable than profits for cyclical businesses.

company_profile.name: ASML Holding N.V.
company_profile.type_rationale: Cyclical business (technology) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
company_profile.description: ASML Holding N.V. provides lithography solutions for the development, production, marketing, sales, upgrading, and servicing of advanced semiconductor equipment systems. The company offers lithography, metrology, and inspection systems. It also provides extreme ultraviolet lithography systems; and deep ultraviolet lithography systems comprising immersion and dry lithography systems solutions to manufacture various range of semiconductor nodes and technologies. In addition, the company offers metrology and inspection systems, including YieldStar optical metrology systems, a diffraction-based wafer metrology platform to assess the quality of patterns on the wafers; and HMI electron beam solutions to locate and analyze individual chip defects. Further, it provides computational lithography solutions, and lithography systems and control software solutions; and refurbishes and upgrades lithography systems, as well as offers customer support and related services. Additionally, the company offers hardware, software, and services to chipmakers to produce the patterns of integrated circuits. It operates in Japan, South Korea, Singapore, Taiwan, China, rest of Asia, the Netherlands, rest of Europe, the Middle East, Africa, and the United States. The company was formerly known as ASM Lithography Holding N.V. and changed its name to ASML Holding N.V. in 2001. ASML Holding N.V. was founded in 1984 and is headquartered in Veldhoven, the Netherlands.
company_profile.description_summary:
This company manufactures advanced lithography equipment used by chipmakers to produce semiconductor patterns. It generates revenue primarily from selling extreme ultraviolet and deep ultraviolet lithography systems, along with metrology and inspection equipment for quality control. It also provides software, refurbishment services, and customer support.

The company distinguishes itself through its computational lithography solutions and specialized electron beam inspection technology for detecting chip defects. Its integrated approach combines hardware, software, and services to help manufacturers produce increasingly advanced semiconductor nodes.

_meta.type_rationale: Cyclical business (technology) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
_meta.method_explanation: Cyclical companies — such as semiconductors, mining, or manufacturing — have profits that rise sharply in good times and fall sharply in downturns. This makes earnings-based methods like P/E unreliable, because the earnings figure at any single point in time can be unusually high or low. EV/EBITDA smooths out some of this distortion by looking at earnings before interest and tax, making it a fairer comparison across the economic cycle. Price-to-Sales is also useful as revenue tends to be more stable than profits for cyclical businesses.

quality.note: Moat scored by Haiku API — final_score_pct computed out of 50.

quality_narrative.moat_rationale: ASML's exceptional 66% return on invested capital and 30% profit margins reflect a fortress competitive position built on irreplaceable semiconductor manufacturing technology that customers cannot easily switch away from.

metric_assessments.profitability: A 10/10 profitability score reflects a 29.7% net profit margin and 52% return on equity—both exceptional figures that far exceed the 8–15% margins and 15–20% ROE that even high-quality businesses typically achieve. The company converts nearly every dollar of revenue into profit, which signals either dominant market position, premium pricing power, or both. The only limiting factor is that semiconductor demand cycles can compress margins during downturns, so this elite profitability level is not permanently guaranteed.
metric_assessments.financial_strength: The 9/10 score is supported by a lean balance sheet with only 0.21x debt-to-EBITDA (well below the 2–3x threshold that signals healthy leverage) and a 1.26 current ratio, meaning ASML has sufficient cash to cover short-term obligations. This combination puts the company in the top tier for financial resilience, even within the cyclical tech sector. The one missing point reflects that a current ratio slightly above 1.0 leaves less cushion than the 1.5+ that ultra-conservative companies maintain, though this is a minor concern given the strong debt position.
metric_assessments.growth: Revenue growth of 13.2% and projected earnings growth of 24.5% over the next five years represent strong momentum, particularly noteworthy for a mature industrial company. The 24.5% EPS growth is well above the 8–12% typical for large-cap tech and signals that profits are expanding faster than sales, driven by operating leverage and improved efficiency. Growth is held to an 8 rather than 9 or 10 because the business remains tethered to semiconductor capital cycles; while management has stated growth is not currently constrained, future demand depends on chip makers' willingness to invest, which swings sharply in cyclical downturns.
metric_assessments.predictability: An 8/10 reflects strong consistency in execution and earnings quality, evidenced by the sustained high ROICs and margins quarter after quarter, plus the visible multi-year EPS forecast of 24.5% CAGR. Customers sign multi-year contracts and depend on ASML's machines for competitive advantage, creating visibility into future demand. However, the semiconductor industry itself remains cyclical—even a fortress business cannot predict when chip makers will slash capital spending, which can derail near-term guidance and create quarters of surprise; this inherent industry volatility caps predictability below 9 or 10.
metric_assessments.valuation: A 2/10 valuation score indicates the stock is trading at a significant premium to intrinsic value, likely reflecting a high price-to-earnings multiple or price-to-book ratio relative to historical averages and peers. Investors are pricing in the strong growth and moat at a steep cost, leaving little margin of safety if earnings disappoint or the semiconductor cycle turns. This is the clearest risk for new investors: buying at today's price requires confidence that the company will deliver the promised 24.5% EPS growth for years, because if growth slows, the premium valuation could compress sharply.

quality_narrative.classification_reason: ASML earned a 'Safe' rating because it combines exceptional business quality (88% overall score driven by market-leading profitability, strong cash generation, and a durable competitive moat) with fortress-like financial strength (minimal debt, low leverage). The rating reflects that this is a high-quality compounder with limited financial risk. However, 'Safe' does not mean 'cheap'—the low valuation score means investors are paying a premium price for that quality, so the safety rating emphasizes business strength rather than opportunity.
quality_narrative.investment_narrative: ASML is the global monopoly supplier of extreme ultraviolet lithography machines, the irreplaceable technology that chip makers need to manufacture the world's most advanced semiconductors, giving it one of the strongest competitive positions in all of technology. The company converts nearly 30% of revenue into net profit and generates a stunning 66% return on every dollar of invested capital—both hallmarks of a business with tremendous pricing power and no real alternative for customers. Growth is accelerating with 24.5% expected earnings expansion over five years, and the balance sheet is fortress-like with minimal debt, so there is no financial distress risk even if the semiconductor cycle weakens. The major downside is valuation: the stock is trading at a steep premium to historical levels, meaning you are paying a full price for this quality and growth, leaving almost no margin of safety if earnings disappoint or chip makers cut capital spending during a downturn. For a beginning investor seeking a genuinely high-quality business with durable competitive advantages and strong long-term growth, ASML is a compelling core holding—but wait for a pullback to a fairer entry price rather than chasing it at current valuations.
quality_narrative.quality_conclusion: ASML Holding N.V. demonstrates strong fundamental quality and scores highly across profitability, financial strength, and predictability, supported by a 66.19% ROIC.
quality_narrative.risk_profile: Key risks to watch: a valuation premium of 48% above estimated intrinsic value.

Because their cash flows are reliable year after year, a Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model works very well. DCF asks: if I add up all the future cash flows this company will generate and adjust for the time value of money, what is it worth today? For these companies, that calculation gives a meaningful and trustworthy answer.

company_profile.description_summary:
Amtech Systems manufactures capital equipment and consumables for semiconductor device fabrication and packaging. _meta.type_rationale: Cyclical business (technology) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
_meta.method_explanation: Cyclical companies — such as semiconductors, mining, or manufacturing — have profits that rise sharply in good times and fall sharply in downturns. This makes earnings-based methods like P/E unreliable, because the earnings figure at any single point in time can be unusually high or low. EV/EBITDA smooths out some of this distortion by looking at earnings before interest and tax, making it a fairer comparison across the economic cycle. Price-to-Sales is also useful as revenue tends to be more stable than profits for cyclical businesses.

Each method approaches value from a different angle — using all of them together gives a more rounded estimate than relying on any single number.

quality_narrative.moat_rationale: Atlantic International has virtually no competitive moat, as evidenced by negative profitability, collapsing margins, and no demonstrated pricing power or customer loyalty to protect earnings.

metric_assessments.profitability: The company is burning cash with a -13.6% profit margin, meaning it loses roughly 14 cents on every dollar of revenue it generates. There is no operational efficiency or cost control visible in the financials, and no signs of a path back to profitability. This is the core problem holding back every other measure of business quality.
metric_assessments.financial_strength: A current ratio of 0.71 means the company has only 71 cents of liquid assets for every dollar of short-term obligations—a red flag for cash flow stress. The absence of return metrics suggests the balance sheet is either heavily leveraged or unprofitable, leaving little cushion if operations worsen further.
metric_assessments.growth: Revenue is contracting at -7.3% annually, and the company shows zero expected earnings growth over the next five years with growth already constrained. This suggests the business is shrinking without any credible plan to reverse course or expand into new markets.
metric_assessments.predictability: Negative margins and shrinking revenue create unpredictable earnings that swing with operational setbacks rather than following a steady trend. Without a stable business model or recurring customer base, forecasting future performance is unreliable.
metric_assessments.valuation: At a 7/10 score, the stock may trade at low multiples simply because it is cheap on paper—not because it represents true value. A low price tag on a deteriorating business is a value trap, not an opportunity, since the discount often fails to compensate for the risk of further declines.

quality_narrative.classification_reason: Atlantic International is classified as Dangerous because it combines operational distress—negative profitability and shrinking revenue—with a weak balance sheet that leaves little room for recovery. The company is losing money on its core business and lacks liquid reserves to weather further headwinds, creating a real risk of financial distress or dilutive financing for shareholders.
quality_narrative.investment_narrative: Atlantic International is a company in visible operational trouble: it is unprofitable, losing 13.6 cents per dollar of sales, and its revenue is contracting year over year with no growth expected in the years ahead. The balance sheet is stretched thin with barely enough cash to cover near-term obligations, leaving almost no buffer if conditions deteriorate further. There are no signs of a turnaround plan, competitive advantage, or market tailwind that would justify confidence in a recovery. The stock's low valuation may feel appealing on the surface, but it reflects the market's justified skepticism about the business's survival and viability. Unless there is a dramatic operational restructuring or acquisition by a stronger player, this company poses significant downside risk; beginners should avoid it in favor of financially healthier alternatives.
quality_narrative.quality_conclusion: Atlantic International Corp. demonstrates weak fundamental quality and is constrained by profitability.
quality_narrative.risk_profile: Key risks to watch: tight liquidity (current ratio 0.71); a weak near-term growth outlook; inconsistent earnings.
quality_narrative.investment_summary: The stock trades at a 52% discount to estimated intrinsic value and is warrants caution — careful position sizing and ongoing monitoring are essential.

_notes.revenue_vs_net_income: This chart shows how much money the company brings in (revenue) versus how much it actually keeps as profit (net income) each year. A healthy company should show both lines growing over time. If revenue grows but net income shrinks, the company is spending more than it is earning — a warning sign worth investigating.
_notes.fcf_vs_debt: Free cash flow is the cash left over after the company has paid for everything it needs to run and grow the business. This chart compares that cash to the company's total debt. A company with rising free cash flow and falling debt is in a strong financial position. If debt is rising faster than cash, the company may be taking on more risk than it can comfortably manage.
_notes.shares_vs_buybacks: When a company buys back its own shares, it reduces the total number of shares in circulation — which means each remaining share represents a larger slice of the business. This chart shows how the share count has changed over time. Consistent buybacks are generally a sign that management believes the stock is undervalued and wants to return cash to shareholders.
_notes.returns_trajectory: Return on Equity (ROE) measures how much profit a company generates for every dollar of shareholder money invested. Return on Invested Capital (ROIC) is similar but also includes debt. Both are expressed as a percentage — higher is better. A company consistently earning above 15% ROIC is generally considered to have a genuine competitive advantage over its peers.
_notes.revenue_vs_ar: Accounts receivable is money the company is owed by customers who have not yet paid their bills. This chart compares revenue growth to accounts receivable growth each year. If accounts receivable is growing much faster than revenue, it may mean the company is struggling to collect payments — sometimes a sign of aggressive or fictitious revenue reporting. A healthy company should see both lines move at a similar pace.
